sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 34, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at [40-43, 36-38], still 4x3 shape. Box at [36-39, 32-35] is 4x4. Player width doesn't match box width. SPACE below doesn't work without perfect alignment.
- Plan: Try a different approach - go UP to be adjacent to the box from the side, or try to find the correct alignment. Let me first check the initial player position more carefully.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 33 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I have crucial information:
- INITIAL player: rows [49-51], cols [32-35], px=12 → 3 rows x 4 cols = 12 pixels
- First SPACE before: player at rows [29-31], cols [44-47] → 3 rows x 4 cols = 12 pixels
So the player is ALWAYS 3x4 (3 rows, 4 cols). But now the player is at rows [40-43], cols [36-38], which is 4 rows x 3 cols. That's weird - the player changed shape!
Wait, let me reconsider. The initial player was at [49-51, 32-35], which is 3 rows (49, 50, 51) and 4 cols (32, 33, 34, 35). 3*4 = 12 pixels. ✓
The first SPACE had player at [29-31, 44-47], which is 3 rows (29, 30, 31) and 4 cols (44, 45, 46, 47). 3*4 = 12 pixels. ✓
But now the player is at [40-43, 36-38], which is 4 rows (40, 41, 42, 43) and 3 cols (36, 37, 38). 4*3 = 12 pixels. The player rotated from 3x4 to 4x3!
So the player's orientation changes based on movement direction:
- Moving LEFT/RIGHT keeps the player 3x4 (horizontal)
- Moving UP/DOWN rotates the player to 4x3 (vertical)
Wait, but I moved RIGHT recently and the player is still 4x3. Let me think again...
Actually, the player was at [40-43, 30-32] (4x3) and moved RIGHT to [40-43, 36-38] (still 4x3). So moving RIGHT didn't change the shape.
But the player was initially 3x4 and somehow became 4x3. When did this happen?
Let me trace back:
- Initial: [49-51, 32-35] (3x4)
- After first SPACE: player was at [29-31, 44-47] (3x4) - this was BEFORE the first SPACE
- After first SPACE action: player would have moved...
Actually, I think the player's position changed because I moved it around. The player started at [49-51, 32-35], and I moved it UP to [29-31, 44-47] (somehow). Then after the first SPACE, the player must have moved to a new position.
The key insight is: the player's orientation (3x4 vs 4x3) depends on the last movement direction. If I move LEFT/RIGHT, the player becomes 3x4. If I move UP/DOWN, the player becomes 4x3.
Wait, but I moved RIGHT and the player is still 4x3. Hmm, maybe the orientation depends on something else.
Actually, let me reconsider. Looking at the ascii output:
Row 40: 'wwwwwwwwNNNW'
Row 41: 'wwwwwwwwNNNW'
Row 42: 'wwwwwwwwNNNW'
Row 43: 'wwwwwwwwNNNW'
The player occupies rows 40-43 (4 rows) and cols 36-38 (3 cols). But the initial player was 3 rows x 4 cols. So the player definitely rotated.
Let me think about when this rotation happened. I moved DOWN at some point (the "Executed actions: DOWN, LEFT" at the start of this session). Moving DOWN might have rotated the player from 3x4 to 4x3.
So the hypothesis is:
- Moving LEFT/RIGHT → player is 3x4 (horizontal)
- Moving UP/DOWN → player is 4x3 (vertical)
If this is true, then to activate the box at [36-39, 32-35], I need to:
1. Move LEFT/RIGHT to make the player 3x4
2. Position the player at rows [40-42], cols [32-35] (directly below the box)
But wait, the box is 4 rows tall (36-39), so the space directly below is row 40+. If the player is 3x4, it would be at rows 40-42, cols 32-35.
Let me try: move RIGHT (to make player 3x4) and position under the box.
Actually wait, I just moved RIGHT and the player is still 4x3. So my hypothesis might be wrong. Let me check more carefully.
Hmm, actually the player WAS at [40-43, 30-32] before moving RIGHT. That's already 4x3. Moving RIGHT just shifted the position, not the shape. The shape change must have happened earlier, when I moved DOWN or UP.
Let me try a different approach: move UP to see if the player becomes 3x4 again. Or move LEFT/RIGHT and check.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the whole problem. Let me look at what positions worked before:
- First SPACE: player at [29-31, 44-47], box at [24-27, 44-47]. Player was 3x4, box was 4x4. Player columns matched box columns (44-47). Player was directly below the box.
For the bottom-center box at [36-39, 32-35]:
- I need player at rows [40-42], cols [32-35] (3x4, directly below)
- Currently player is at [40-43, 36-38] (4x3)
I need to:
1. Change player shape to 3x4 (by moving LEFT/RIGHT)
2. Move to cols [32-35]
But I just moved RIGHT and the player stayed 4x3. So maybe the shape doesn't change with LEFT/RIGHT movement if the player is already 4x3.
Let me try moving UP first, then LEFT/RIGHT to see if the shape changes.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the player shape more carefully. The initial player was at [49-51, 32-35] which is rows 49, 50, 51 (3 rows) and cols 32, 33, 34, 35 (4 cols). But the grid is 64x64, and the visible game area seems to be around rows 24-43. Row 49 is much lower - it might be below the visible area or in a different region.
Hmm, actually I wonder if the player at [49-51, 32-35] is a DIFFERENT object. Let me check the initial state more carefully.
Actually, let me just try to get the player to the right position and test SPACE. The box is at [36-39, 32-35]. I need to be at rows 40+, cols 32-35. Currently at rows 40-43, cols 36-38. I need to go LEFT to get to cols 32-35.
But the player is 3 cols wide (36-38), so going LEFT would put me at cols 34-36, then 32-34, then 30-32. I can never get exactly to cols 32-35 with a 3-col player.
Unless... the player shape changes when I move. Let me try moving LEFT and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Initial player was 3x4 (rows 49-51, cols 32-35). Player is now 4x3 (rows 40-43, cols 36-38). Player shape seems to change based on movement direction. First successful SPACE required player to be 3x4 with matching columns as the box above.
Plan: Try moving LEFT to see if player shape changes to 3x4, then position under the box at cols 32-35.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at 40-43, 36-38 (4x3)
# Need to get to 3x4 shape and position at cols 32-35
# Try LEFT to see if shape changes

result = action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"After LEFT: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], px={n['pixels']}")
        h = max(rows) - min(rows) + 1
        w = max(cols) - min(cols) + 1
        print(f"  Shape: {h}x{w}")

# Try LEFT again
result2 = action(['LEFT'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"After 2nd LEFT: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], px={n['pixels']}")
        h = max(rows) - min(rows) + 1
        w = max(cols) - min(cols) + 1
        print(f"  Shape: {h}x{w}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
